metadata.title:
USE OF CARLISLE
QUESI!0NEDBY


HOUSE

REPRESENTATIV SAY GRAD
YATES OF INDIAN SCHOOL
RETURN TO BLANKETS


AND TEPEE.

pages.raw_text:
WASHINGTON, Feb. lbs the
Carlisle Indian school doing any
good besides training several husky
football players every year?


Do the graduates become useful
citizens or do they return to the
blanket and tepee and sleep with
dogs as their aboriginal ancestors
have done for generation?


These were the questions dis
cussed in the house this afternoon
during consideration of the indian
appropriation bill.


Usually this measure, carrying
eight or nine million dollars for
the support of the indians and for
the carrying out of treaty obliga-
tons made with the tribemen in
the past, goes through as though it
were creased.


This year half the members on
the floor seem anxious to take a
whack at it.


The greater part of today's de-
bate was upon the subject of the
appropriations for the Carlisle and
other schools. Representative
Tawney Republican, Minnesota
led the attack on this item, declar-
ing he had his doubts about the
good accomplished by the Indian
schools. He himself had visited a
little town near Albuquerque. n. NJ,
and there had seen the seven Car
Isle graduates which the town
boa Stad Nome of them WOI1ld taIk
English and all had gone back to
the blanket, the moccasin and the
dogs.
